revision.text:
{{family name hatnote|Soga|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | honorific_prefix = | name = Soga no Umako | honorific_suffix = | native_name = 蘇我 馬子 | native_name_lang = jpn | image = | image_size = | alt = | caption = | years_active = late 6th century – early 7th century | known_for = Political reforms of [[Asuka period]], associate of [[Prince Shōtoku]], promoter of [[Buddhism]] | birth_name = | birth_date = 551? | birth_place = | death_date = {{Death date|626|6|19|mf=yes}} | death_place = | death_cause = | resting_place = Shimanoshō, [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], [[Nara Prefecture]], [[Japan]] (traditionally) | resting_place_coordinates = {{Coord|34|28|0.7|N|135|49|34.1|E|display=title}} | monuments = [[Ishibutai Kofun]] (traditionally) | other_names = {{Nihongo|Shima no Ōomi|嶋大臣}} | spouse = Daughter of [[Mononobe no Ogushi]] | partner = | children = [[Kahiiko no Iratsume]]
[[Soga no Emishi]]
Soga no Kuramaro
Tojiko no Iratsume
Hode no Iratsume | parents = [[Soga no Iname]] | relatives = }} [[File:IshibutaiBlueStairsScan3.jpg|thumb|right|[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is considered likely to have been intended as the tomb of Soga no Umako.]] {{nihongo|'''Soga no Umako'''|蘇我 馬子||551? – June 19, 626June 19, 626 corresponds to the twentieth day of the fifth month of 626 ([[Sexagenary cycle|Heibo]]) of the traditional [[lunisolar calendar]] used in Japan until 1873}} was the son of [[Soga no Iname]] and a member of the powerful [[Soga clan]] of [[Japan]]. Umako conducted political reforms with [[Prince Shōtoku]] during the rules of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] and [[Empress Suiko]]{{cite book |author=Mulhern, Chieko Irie |title=Heroic with grace: legendary women of Japan |publisher=M.E. Sharpe |location=Armonk, N.Y |year=1991 |page=40 |isbn=0-87332-552-4 }} and established the Soga clan's stronghold in the government by having his daughters married to members of the imperial family. In the late 6th century, Soga no Umako went to great lengths to promote Buddhism in Japan, and was instrumental in its acceptance. At that time, the Soga clan employed immigrants from China and Korea, and worked to obtain advanced technology and other knowledge. In 587, Umako defeated [[Mononobe no Moriya]] in the [[Soga–Mononobe conflict|Battle of Shigisan]], securing Soga dominance. On January 15, 593, relics of Buddha Shakyamuni were deposited inside the foundation stone under the pillar of a pagoda at [[Asuka-dera]] (Hōkō-ji at the time), a temple whose construction Umako ordered, according to the Suiko section of the [[Nihonshoki]].{{cite book |author=Aston, W. G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }} [[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}} ==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children. *[[Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[Emperor Jomei]] ==References== {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Soga no, Umako}} [[Category:Soga clan]] [[Category:626 deaths]] [[Category:People of Asuka-period Japan]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Asuka period]] [[Category:Year of birth uncertain]] {{Japan-bio-stub}}

On January 15, 593, relics of Buddha Shakyamuni were deposited inside the foundation stone under the pillar of a pagoda at [[Asuka-dera]] (Hōkō-ji at the time), a temple whose construction Umako ordered, according to the Suiko section of the [[Nihonshoki]].{{cite book |author=Aston, W. G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }} [[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}} ==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children. *[[Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[Emperor Jomei]] ==References== {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Soga no, Umako}} [[Category:Soga clan]] [[Category:626 deaths]] [[Category:People of Asuka-period Japan]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Asuka period]] [[Category:Year of birth uncertain]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia4]] [[en:Soga no Umako]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia4]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia3]] [[Category:pages from wikipedia]][[en:Soga no Umako]][[Category:pages from wikipedia]] {{Japan-bio-stub}}

G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }} [[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}} ==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children. *[[Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[Emperor Jomei]] ==References== {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Soga no, Umako}} [[Category:Soga clan]] [[Category:626 deaths]] [[Category:People of Asuka-period Japan]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Asuka period]] [[Category:Year of birth uncertain]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia41]] [[en:Soga no Umako]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia41]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia03]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia23]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Soga no Umako]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia23]] [[Category:dd]] {{Japan-bio-stub}}

Tojiko no Iratsume
Hode no Iratsume | parents = [[Soga no Iname]] | relatives = }} [[File:IshibutaiBlueStairsScan3.jpg|thumb|right|[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is considered likely to have been intended as the tomb of Soga no Umako.]] {{nihongo|'''Soga no Umako'''|蘇我 馬子||551? – June 19, 626June 19, 626 corresponds to the twentieth day of the fifth month of 626 ([[Sexagenary cycle|Heibo]]) of the traditional [[lunisolar calendar]] used in Japan until 1873}} was the son of [[Soga no Iname]] and a member of the powerful [[Soga clan]] of [[Japan]]. Umako conducted political reforms with [[Prince Shōtoku]] during the rules of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] and [[Empress Suiko]]{{cite book |author=Mulhern, Chieko Irie |title=Heroic with grace: legendary women of Japan |publisher=M.E. Sharpe |location=Armonk, N.Y |year=1991 |page=40 |isbn=0-87332-552-4 }} and established the Soga clan's stronghold in the government by having his daughters married to members of the imperial family. In the late 6th century, Soga no Umako went to great lengths to promote Buddhism in Japan, and was instrumental in its acceptance. At that time, the Soga clan employed immigrants from China and Korea, and worked to obtain advanced technology and other knowledge. In 587, Umako defeated [[Mononobe no Moriya]] in the [[Soga–Mononobe conflict|Battle of Shigisan]], securing Soga dominance. On January 15, 593, relics of Buddha Shakyamuni were deposited inside the foundation stone under the pillar of a pagoda at [[Asuka-dera]] (Hōkō-ji at the time), a temple whose construction Umako ordered, according to the Suiko section of the [[Nihonshoki]].{{cite book |author=Aston, W. G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }} [[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}} ==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children. *[[Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[Emperor Jomei]] ==References== {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Soga no, Umako}} [[Category:Soga clan]] [[Category:626 deaths]] [[Category:People of Asuka-period Japan]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Asuka period]] [[Category:Year of birth uncertain]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia41]] [[en:Soga no Umako]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia41]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia06]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia23]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Soga no Umako]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia23]] [[Category:dd]] {{Japan-bio-stub}}

Sharpe |location=Armonk, N.Y |year=1991 |page=40 |isbn=0-87332-552-4 }} and established the Soga clan's stronghold in the government by having his daughters married to members of the imperial family. In the late 6th century, Soga no Umako went to great lengths to promote Buddhism in Japan, and was instrumental in its acceptance. At that time, the Soga clan employed immigrants from China and Korea, and worked to obtain advanced technology and other knowledge. In 587, Umako defeated [[Mononobe no Moriya]] in the [[Soga–Mononobe conflict|Battle of Shigisan]], securing Soga dominance. On January 15, 593, relics of Buddha Shakyamuni were deposited inside the foundation stone under the pillar of a pagoda at [[Asuka-dera]] (Hōkō-ji at the time), a temple whose construction Umako ordered, according to the Suiko section of the [[Nihonshoki]].{{cite book |author=Aston, W. G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }} [[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}} ==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children. *[[Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[Emperor Jomei]] ==References== {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Soga no, Umako}} [[category:kk]][[category:Soga clan]] [[category:kk]][[category:626 deaths]] [[category:kk]][[category:People of Asuka-period Japan]] [[category:kk]][[category:Buddhism in the Asuka period]] [[category:kk]][[category:Year of birth uncertain]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Soga no Umako]]]] [[category:mm]] [[en:Soga no Umako]][[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Soga no Umako]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Soga no Umako]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Soga no Umako]]]] [[category:mm]] [[en:Soga no Umako]][[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Soga no Umako]]]] [[category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Soga no Umako]][[Category:dd]] {{Japan-bio-stub}}

Umako conducted political reforms with [[Prince Shōtoku]] during the rules of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] and [[Empress Suiko]]{{cite book |author=Mulhern, Chieko Irie |title=Heroic with grace: legendary women of Japan |publisher=M.E. Sharpe |location=Armonk, N.Y |year=1991 |page=40 |isbn=0-87332-552-4 }} and established the Soga clan's stronghold in the government by having his daughters married to members of the imperial family. they had five children. *[[Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[Emperor Jomei]] ==References== [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Soga no Umako]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Soga no, Umako}} [[Category:Soga clan]] [[Category:626 deaths]] [[Category:People of Asuka-period Japan]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Asuka period]] [[Category:Year of birth uncertain]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:11 6]] [[en:Soga no Umako]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Soga no Umako]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:11 6]] [[en:Soga no Umako]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:11 6]] [[en:Soga no Umako]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:11 6]] [[en:Soga no Umako]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Soga no Umako]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:11 6]] [[en:Soga no Umako]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:11]][[Category:Soga no Umako]][[Category:11]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[en:Soga no Umako]][[Category:kk]][[en:Soga no Umako]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]][[en:Soga no Umako]][[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[en:Soga no Umako]]{{Japan-bio-stub}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Soga no Umako]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }} [[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}} ==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children. *[[Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[Emperor Jomei]] ==References== ]] ]] ]] ]] ]] {{Japan-bio-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Soga clan]] [[Category:626 deaths]] [[Category:People of Asuka-period Japan]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Asuka period]] [[Category:Year of birth uncertain]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11 6]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Soga no Umako]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[en:Soga no Umako]] [[sn:Soga no Umako]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Soga no, Umako}}

G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }} [[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}} ==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children. *[[Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[Emperor Jomei]] ==References== ]] ]] {{Japan-bio-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Soga clan]] [[Category:626 deaths]] [[Category:People of Asuka-period Japan]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Asuka period]] [[Category:Year of birth uncertain]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Soga no Umako]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[en:Soga no Umako]] [[sn:Soga no Umako]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Soga no, Umako}}

Hode no Iratsume | parents = [[Soga no Iname]] | relatives = }} [[File:IshibutaiBlueStairsScan3.jpg|thumb|right|[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is considered likely to have been intended as the tomb of Soga no Umako.]] {{nihongo|'''Soga no Umako'''|蘇我 馬子||551? – June 19, 626June 19, 626 corresponds to the twentieth day of the fifth month of 626 ([[Sexagenary cycle|Heibo]]) of the traditional [[lunisolar calendar]] used in Japan until 1873}} was the son of [[Soga no Iname]] and a member of the powerful [[Soga clan]] of [[Japan]]. Umako conducted political reforms with [[Prince Shōtoku]] during the rules of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] and [[Empress Suiko]]{{cite book |author=Mulhern, Chieko Irie |title=Heroic with grace: legendary women of Japan |publisher=M.E. Sharpe |location=Armonk, N.Y |year=1991 |page=40 |isbn=0-87332-552-4 }} and established the Soga clan's stronghold in the government by having his daughters married to members of the imperial family. In the late 6th century, Soga no Umako went to great lengths to promote Buddhism in Japan, and was instrumental in its acceptance. At that time, the Soga clan employed immigrants from China and Korea, and worked to obtain advanced technology and other knowledge. In 587, Umako defeated [[Mononobe no Moriya]] in the [[Soga–Mononobe conflict|Battle of Shigisan]], securing Soga dominance. On January 15, 593, relics of Buddha Shakyamuni were deposited inside the foundation stone under the pillar of a pagoda at [[Asuka-dera]] (Hōkō-ji at the time), a temple whose construction Umako ordered, according to the Suiko section of the [[Nihonshoki]].{{cite book |author=Aston, W. G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }} [[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}} ==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children. *[[Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[Emperor Jomei]] ==References== ]] ]] {{Japan-bio-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Soga clan]] [[Category:626 deaths]] [[Category:People of Asuka-period Japan]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Asuka period]] [[Category:Year of birth uncertain]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Soga no Umako]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[en:Soga no Umako]] [[sn:Soga no Umako]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Soga no, Umako}}

G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }} [[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}} ==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children. *[[Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[Emperor Jomei]] ==References== ]] ]] {{Japan-bio-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Soga no, Umako}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Soga clan]] [[Category:626 deaths]] [[Category:People of Asuka-period Japan]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Asuka period]] [[Category:Year of birth uncertain]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Soga no Umako]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Soga no Umako]] [[en:Soga no Umako]]

Soga no Kuramaro
Tojiko no Iratsume
Hode no Iratsume | parents = [[Soga no Iname]] | relatives = }} [[File:IshibutaiBlueStairsScan3.jpg|thumb|right|[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is considered likely to have been intended as the tomb of Soga no Umako.]] {{nihongo|'''Soga no Umako'''|蘇我 馬子||551? – June 19, 626June 19, 626 corresponds to the twentieth day of the fifth month of 626 ([[Sexagenary cycle|Heibo]]) of the traditional [[lunisolar calendar]] used in Japan until 1873}} was the son of [[Soga no Iname]] and a member of the powerful [[Soga clan]] of [[Japan]]. Umako conducted political reforms with [[Prince Shōtoku]] during the rules of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] and [[Empress Suiko]]{{cite book |author=Mulhern, Chieko Irie |title=Heroic with grace: legendary women of Japan |publisher=M.E. Sharpe |location=Armonk, N.Y |year=1991 |page=40 |isbn=0-87332-552-4 }} and established the Soga clan's stronghold in the government by having his daughters married to members of the imperial family. In the late 6th century, Soga no Umako went to great lengths to promote Buddhism in Japan, and was instrumental in its acceptance. At that time, the Soga clan employed immigrants from China and Korea, and worked to obtain advanced technology and other knowledge. In 587, Umako defeated [[Mononobe no Moriya]] in the [[Soga–Mononobe conflict|Battle of Shigisan]], securing Soga dominance. On January 15, 593, relics of Buddha Shakyamuni were deposited inside the foundation stone under the pillar of a pagoda at [[Asuka-dera]] (Hōkō-ji at the time), a temple whose construction Umako ordered, according to the Suiko section of the [[Nihonshoki]].{{cite book |author=Aston, W. G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }} [[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}} ==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children. *[[Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[Emperor Jomei]] ==References== ]] ]] {{Japan-bio-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Soga clan]] [[Category:626 deaths]] [[Category:People of Asuka-period Japan]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Asuka period]] [[Category:Year of birth uncertain]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Soga no Umako]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Soga no Umako]] [[en:Soga no Umako]]

{{family name hatnote|Soga|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | honorific_prefix = | name = Soga no Umako | honorific_suffix = | native_name = 蘇我 馬子 | native_name_lang = jpn | image = | image_size = | alt = | caption = | years_active = late 6th century – early 7th century | known_for = Political reforms of [[Asuka period]], associate of [[Prince Shōtoku]], promoter of [[Buddhism]] | birth_name = | birth_date = 551? | birth_place = | death_date = {{Death date|626|6|19|mf=yes}} | death_place = | death_cause = | resting_place = Shimanoshō, [[Asuka, Nara|Asuka]], [[Nara Prefecture]], [[Japan]] (traditionally) | resting_place_coordinates = {{Coord|34|28|0.7|N|135|49|34.1|E|display=title}} | monuments = [[Ishibutai Kofun]] (traditionally) | other_names = {{Nihongo|Shima no Ōomi|嶋大臣}} | spouse = Daughter of [[Mononobe no Ogushi]] | partner = | children = [[Kahiiko no Iratsume]]
[[Soga no Emishi]]
Soga no Kuramaro
Tojiko no Iratsume
Hode no Iratsume | parents = [[Soga no Iname]] | relatives = }} [[File:IshibutaiBlueStairsScan3.jpg|thumb|right|[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is considered likely to have been intended as the tomb of Soga no Umako.]] {{nihongo|'''Soga no Umako'''|蘇我 馬子||551? – June 19, 626June 19, 626 corresponds to the twentieth day of the fifth month of 626 ([[Sexagenary cycle|Heibo]]) of the traditional [[lunisolar calendar]] used in Japan until 1873}} was the son of [[Soga no Iname]] and a member of the powerful [[Soga clan]] of [[Japan]]. Umako conducted political reforms with [[Prince Shōtoku]] during the rules of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] and [[Empress Suiko]]{{cite book |author=Mulhern, Chieko Irie |title=Heroic with grace: legendary women of Japan |publisher=M.E. Sharpe |location=Armonk, N.Y |year=1991 |page=40 |isbn=0-87332-552-4 }} and established the Soga clan's stronghold in the government by having his daughters married to members of the imperial family. In the late 6th century, Soga no Umako went to great lengths to promote Buddhism in Japan, and was instrumental in its acceptance. At that time, the Soga clan employed immigrants from China and Korea, and worked to obtain advanced technology and other knowledge. In 587, Umako defeated [[Mononobe no Moriya]] in the [[Soga–Mononobe conflict|Battle of Shigisan]], securing Soga dominance. On January 15, 593, relics of Buddha Shakyamuni were deposited inside the foundation stone under the pillar of a pagoda at [[Asuka-dera]] (Hōkō-ji at the time), a temple whose construction Umako ordered, according to the Suiko section of the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]].{{cite book |author=Aston, W. G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }} [[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}} ==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children. *[[Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[Emperor Jomei]] ==References== ]] ]] {{Japan-bio-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Soga clan]] [[Category:626 deaths]] [[Category:People of Asuka-period Japan]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Asuka period]] [[Category:Year of birth uncertain]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Soga no Umako]] [[en:Soga no Umako]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]]

In the late 6th century, Soga no Umako went to great lengths to promote Buddhism in Japan, and was instrumental in its acceptance. At that time, the Soga clan employed immigrants from China and Korea, and worked to obtain advanced technology and other knowledge. In 587, Umako defeated [[Mononobe no Moriya]] in the [[Soga–Mononobe conflict|Battle of Shigisan]], securing Soga dominance. G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }} [[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}} ==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children. *[[Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[Emperor Jomei]] ==References== ]] ]] {{Japan-bio-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Soga clan]] [[Category:626 deaths]] [[Category:People of Asuka-period Japan]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Asuka period]] [[Category:Year of birth uncertain]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Soga no Umako]] [[en:Soga no Umako]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] [[ar:سوغا نو أوماكو]] [[bn:সোগা নো উমাকো]] [[ca:Soga no Umako]] [[de:Soga no Umako]] [[es:Soga no Umako]] [[fa:سوگا نو اوماکو]] [[fr:Soga no Umako]] [[it:Soga no Umako]] [[ja:蘇我馬子]] [[ko:소가노 우마코]] [[nl:Soga no Umako]] [[pl:Umako Soga]] [[pt:Soga no Umako]] [[ru:Сога-но Умако]] [[th:โซงะ โนะ อูมาโกะ]] [[tr:Soga no Umako]] [[uk:Соґа но Умако]] [[vi:Soga no Umako]] [[zh:蘇我馬子]] [[zh_classical:蘇我馬子]] [[zh_min_nan:Soga no Umako]] [[zh_yue:蘇我馬子]]

On January 15, 593, relics of Buddha Shakyamuni were deposited inside the foundation stone under the pillar of a pagoda at [[:en:Asuka-dera|Asuka-dera]] (Hōkō-ji at the time), a temple whose construction Umako ordered, according to the Suiko section of the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]].{{cite book |author=Aston, W. G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }} [[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}} ==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children. *[[Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[Emperor Jomei]] ==References== ]] ]] {{Japan-bio-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Soga clan]] [[Category:626 deaths]] [[Category:People of Asuka-period Japan]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Asuka period]] [[Category:Year of birth uncertain]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Soga no Umako]] [[en:Soga no Umako]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] [[ar:سوغا نو أوماكو]] [[bn:সোগা নো উমাকো]] [[ca:Soga no Umako]] [[de:Soga no Umako]] [[es:Soga no Umako]] [[fa:سوگا نو اوماکو]] [[fr:Soga no Umako]] [[it:Soga no Umako]] [[ja:蘇我馬子]] [[ko:소가노 우마코]] [[nl:Soga no Umako]] [[pl:Umako Soga]] [[pt:Soga no Umako]] [[ru:Сога-но Умако]] [[th:โซงะ โนะ อูมาโกะ]] [[tr:Soga no Umako]] [[uk:Соґа но Умако]] [[vi:Soga no Umako]] [[zh:蘇我馬子]] [[zh_classical:蘇我馬子]] [[zh-min-nan:Soga no Umako]] [[zh-yue:蘇我馬子]]

G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }} [[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}} ==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children. *[[Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Emperor Jomei|Emperor Jomei]] ==References== ]] ]] {{Japan-bio-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Soga clan]] [[Category:626 deaths]] [[Category:People of Asuka-period Japan]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Asuka period]] [[Category:Year of birth uncertain]] }} [[sn:Soga no Umako]] [[en:Soga no Umako]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] [[ar:سوغا نو أوماكو]] [[bn:সোগা নো উমাকো]] [[ca:Soga no Umako]] [[de:Soga no Umako]] [[es:Soga no Umako]] [[fa:سوگا نو اوماکو]] [[fr:Soga no Umako]] [[it:Soga no Umako]] [[ja:蘇我馬子]] [[ko:소가노 우마코]] [[nl:Soga no Umako]] [[pl:Umako Soga]] [[pt:Soga no Umako]] [[ru:Сога-но Умако]] [[th:โซงะ โนะ อูมาโกะ]] [[tr:Soga no Umako]] [[uk:Соґа но Умако]] [[vi:Soga no Umako]] [[zh:蘇我馬子]] [[zh_classical:蘇我馬子]] [[zh-min-nan:Soga no Umako]] [[zh-yue:蘇我馬子]]

Umako conducted political reforms with [[Prince Shōtoku]] during the rules of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] and [[Empress Suiko]]{{cite book |author=Mulhern, Chieko Irie |title=Heroic with grace: legendary women of Japan |publisher=M.E. Sharpe |location=Armonk, N.Y |year=1991 |page=40 |isbn=0-87332-552-4 }} and established the Soga clan's stronghold in the government by having his daughters married to members of the imperial family. In the late 6th century, Soga no Umako went to great lengths to promote Buddhism in Japan, and was instrumental in its acceptance. At that time, the Soga clan employed immigrants from China and Korea, and worked to obtain advanced technology and other knowledge. In 587, Umako defeated [[Mononobe no Moriya]] in the [[Soga–Mononobe conflict|Battle of Shigisan]], securing Soga dominance. On January 15, 593, relics of Buddha Shakyamuni were deposited inside the foundation stone under the pillar of a pagoda at [[:en:Asuka-dera|Asuka-dera]] (Hōkō-ji at the time), a temple whose construction Umako ordered, according to the Suiko section of the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]].{{cite book |author=Aston, W. G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }} [[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}} ==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children. *[[Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[:en:Emperor Sushun|Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Emperor Jomei|Emperor Jomei]] ==References== ]] ]] {{Japan-bio-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Soga clan]] [[Category:626 deaths]] [[Category:People of Asuka-period Japan]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Asuka period]] [[Category:Year of birth uncertain]] }} [[sn:Soga no Umako]] [[en:Soga no Umako]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] [[ar:سوغا نو أوماكو]] [[bn:সোগা নো উমাকো]] [[ca:Soga no Umako]] [[de:Soga no Umako]] [[es:Soga no Umako]] [[fa:سوگا نو اوماکو]] [[fr:Soga no Umako]] [[it:Soga no Umako]] [[ja:蘇我馬子]] [[ko:소가노 우마코]] [[nl:Soga no Umako]] [[pl:Umako Soga]] [[pt:Soga no Umako]] [[ru:Сога-но Умако]] [[th:โซงะ โนะ อูมาโกะ]] [[tr:Soga no Umako]] [[uk:Соґа но Умако]] [[vi:Soga no Umako]] [[zh:蘇我馬子]] [[zh_classical:蘇我馬子]] [[zh-min-nan:Soga no Umako]] [[zh-yue:蘇我馬子]]

G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }} [[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}} ==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children. *[[Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[:en:Emperor Sushun|Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Emperor Jomei|Emperor Jomei]] ==References== ]] ]] {{Japan-bio-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Soga clan]] [[Category:626 deaths]] [[Category:People of Asuka-period Japan]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Asuka period]] [[Category:Year of birth uncertain]] }} [[sn:Soga no Umako]] [[en:Soga no Umako]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] [[ar:سوغا نو أوماكو]] [[bn:সোগা নো উমাকো]] [[ca:Soga no Umako]] [[de:Soga no Umako]] [[es:Soga no Umako]] [[fa:سوگا نو اوماکو]] [[fr:Soga no Umako]] [[it:Soga no Umako]] [[ja:蘇我馬子]] [[ko:소가노 우마코]] [[nl:Soga no Umako]] [[pl:Umako Soga]] [[pt:Soga no Umako]] [[ru:Сога-но Умако]] [[th:โซงะ โนะ อูมาโกะ]] [[tr:Soga no Umako]] [[uk:Соґа но Умако]] [[vi:Soga no Umako]] [[zh:蘇我馬子]] [[zh-classical:蘇我馬子]] [[zh-min-nan:Soga no Umako]] [[zh-yue:蘇我馬子]]

G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }} [[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}} ==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children. *[[Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[:en:Emperor Sushun|Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Emperor Jomei|Emperor Jomei]] ==References== ]] ]] {{Japan-bio-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Soga clan]] [[Category:626 deaths]] [[Category:People of Asuka-period Japan]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Asuka period]] [[Category:Year of birth uncertain]] }} [[sn:Soga no Umako]] [[en:Soga no Umako]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[ar:سوغا نو أوماكو]] [[bn:সোগা নো উমাকো]] [[ca:Soga no Umako]] [[de:Soga no Umako]] [[es:Soga no Umako]] [[fa:سوگا نو اوماکو]] [[fr:Soga no Umako]] [[it:Soga no Umako]] [[ja:蘇我馬子]] [[ko:소가노 우마코]] [[nl:Soga no Umako]] [[pl:Umako Soga]] [[pt:Soga no Umako]] [[ru:Сога-но Умако]] [[th:โซงะ โนะ อูมาโกะ]] [[tr:Soga no Umako]] [[uk:Соґа но Умако]] [[vi:Soga no Umako]] [[zh:蘇我馬子]] [[zh-classical:蘇我馬子]] [[zh-min-nan:Soga no Umako]] [[zh-yue:蘇我馬子]]

Umako conducted political reforms with [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] during the rules of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] and [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]]{{cite book |author=Mulhern, Chieko Irie |title=Heroic with grace: legendary women of Japan |publisher=M.E. Sharpe |location=Armonk, N.Y |year=1991 |page=40 |isbn=0-87332-552-4 }} and established the Soga clan's stronghold in the government by having his daughters married to members of the imperial family. In the late 6th century, Soga no Umako went to great lengths to promote Buddhism in Japan, and was instrumental in its acceptance. At that time, the Soga clan employed immigrants from China and Korea, and worked to obtain advanced technology and other knowledge. In 587, Umako defeated [[:en:Mononobe no Moriya|Mononobe no Moriya]] in the [[Soga–Mononobe conflict|Battle of Shigisan]], securing Soga dominance. On January 15, 593, relics of Buddha Shakyamuni were deposited inside the foundation stone under the pillar of a pagoda at [[:en:Asuka-dera|Asuka-dera]] (Hōkō-ji at the time), a temple whose construction Umako ordered, according to the Suiko section of the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]].{{cite book |author=Aston, W. In 587, Umako defeated [[:en:Mononobe no Moriya|Mononobe no Moriya]] in the [[:en:Soga–Mononobe conflict|Battle of Shigisan]], securing Soga dominance. On January 15, 593, relics of Buddha Shakyamuni were deposited inside the foundation stone under the pillar of a pagoda at [[:en:Asuka-dera|Asuka-dera]] (Hōkō-ji at the time), a temple whose construction Umako ordered, according to the Suiko section of the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]].{{cite book |author=Aston, W. G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }} [[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}} ==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children. *[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[:en:Emperor Sushun|Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Shotoku Taishi|Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Emperor Jomei|Emperor Jomei]] ==References== ]]{{Japan-bio-stub}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Soga clan]] [[Category:626 deaths]] [[Category:People of Asuka-period Japan]] [[Category:Buddhism in the Asuka period]] [[Category:Year of birth uncertain]] }} [[sn:Soga no Umako]] [[en:Soga no Umako]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[ar:سوغا نو أوماكو]] [[bn:সোগা নো উমাকো]] [[ca:Soga no Umako]] [[de:Soga no Umako]] [[es:Soga no Umako]] [[fa:سوگا نو اوماکو]] [[fr:Soga no Umako]] [[it:Soga no Umako]] [[ja:蘇我馬子]] [[ko:소가노 우마코]] [[nl:Soga no Umako]] [[pl:Umako Soga]] [[pt:Soga no Umako]] [[ru:Сога-но Умако]] [[th:โซงะ โนะ อูมาโกะ]] [[tr:Soga no Umako]] [[uk:Соґа но Умако]] [[vi:Soga no Umako]] [[zh:蘇我馬子]] [[zh-classical:蘇我馬子]] [[zh-min-nan:Soga no Umako]] [[zh-yue:蘇我馬子]]

Umako conducted political reforms with [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] during the rules of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] and [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]]<ref>{{cite book |author=Mulhern, Chieko Irie |title=Heroic with grace: legendary women of Japan |publisher=M.E. Sharpe |location=Armonk, N.Y |year=1991 |page=40 |isbn=0-87332-552-4 }}</ref> and established the Soga clan's stronghold in the government by having his daughters married to members of the imperial family.

In 587, Umako defeated [[:en:Mononobe no Moriya|Mononobe no Moriya]] in the [[:en:Soga–Mononobe conflict|Battle of Shigisan]], securing Soga dominance. On January 15, 593, relics of Buddha Shakyamuni Tathāgata were deposited inside the foundation stone under the pillar of a pagoda at [[:en:Asuka-dera|Asuka-dera]] (Hōkō-ji at the time), a temple whose construction Umako ordered, according to the Suiko section of the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]].<ref>{{cite book |author=Aston, W. G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }}</ref>
[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}}</ref>
==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children.
*[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[:en:Emperor Sushun|Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Shotoku Taishi|Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Emperor Jomei|Emperor Jomei]]

[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}}</ref>
==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children.
*[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[:en:Emperor Sushun|Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Shotoku Taishi|Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Emperor Jomei|Emperor Jomei]]

G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }}</ref>

{{ill|Kamaashi-hime|qid=Q106118985}} | partner = | children = {{ill|Kawakami no Iratsume|qid=Q11553322}}; {{ill|Q106095528|qid=Q106095528}}; {{ill|Soga no Emishi|qid=Q1292368}}; {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|qid=Q11625195}}; {{ill|Soga no Zentoko|qid=Q11625196}}; {{ill|Tojiko no Iratsume|qid=Q11396062}} | parents = [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] | relatives ={{ill|Oane-no-kimi|qid=Q11625199}}; {{ill|Sakaibe no Marise|qid=Q7402774}}; {{ill|Soga no Kitashihime|qid=Q7554458}} | gender = {{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}} | nationality = {{ill|Japan|qid=Q17}} | occupation = {{ill|politician|qid=Q82955}} | title = {{ill|Ōomi|qid=Q4328809}} | father = {{ill|Soga no Iname|qid=Q595897}} | family = {{ill|Soga clan|qid=Q909854}} | notable_works = {{ill|Kokki|qid=Q826177}}; {{ill|Omi Muraji Tomo no Miyatsuko Kuni no Miyatsuko Momo Amari Yaso Tomonoo Awasete Ōmi Takara Domo no Moto tsu Fumi|qid=Q11612432}}; {{ill|Tennōki|qid=Q6141029}} }} [[File:IshibutaiBlueStairsScan3.jpg|thumb|right|[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is considered likely to have been intended as the tomb of Soga no Umako.]] {{nihongo|'''Soga no Umako'''|蘇我 馬子||551? – June 19, 626<ref>June 19, 626 corresponds to the twentieth day of the fifth month of 626 ([[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Heibo]]) of the traditional [[:en:en:Lunisolar calendar|en:Lunisolar calendar]] used in Japan until 1873</ref>}} was the son of [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] and a member of the powerful [[Soga clan]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
Umako conducted political reforms with [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] during the rules of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] and [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]]<ref>{{cite book |author=Mulhern, Chieko Irie |title=Heroic with grace: legendary women of Japan |publisher=M.E. Sharpe |location=Armonk, N.Y |year=1991 |page=40 |isbn=0-87332-552-4 }}</ref> and established the Soga clan's stronghold in the government by having his daughters married to members of the imperial family.
In the late 6th century, Soga no Umako went to great lengths to promote Buddhism in Japan, and was instrumental in its acceptance. At that time, the Soga clan employed immigrants from China and Korea, and worked to obtain advanced technology and other knowledge. In 587, Umako defeated [[:en:Mononobe no Moriya|Mononobe no Moriya]] in the [[:en:Soga–Mononobe conflict|Battle of Shigisan]], securing Soga dominance. On January 15, 593, relics of Buddha Shakyamuni Tathāgata were deposited inside the foundation stone under the pillar of a pagoda at [[:en:Asuka-dera|Asuka-dera]] (Hōkō-ji at the time), a temple whose construction Umako ordered, according to the Suiko section of the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]].<ref>{{cite book |author=Aston, W. G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }}</ref>
[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}}</ref>
==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children.
*[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[:en:Emperor Sushun|Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Shotoku Taishi|Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Emperor Jomei|Emperor Jomei]]
==References== <references/>]]{{Japan-bio-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:سوغا نو أوماكو]][[bn:সোগা নো উমাকো]][[ca:Soga no Umako]][[de:Soga no Umako]][[en:Soga no Umako]][[es:Soga no Umako]][[fa:سوگا نو اوماکو]][[fr:Soga no Umako]][[it:Soga no Umako]][[ja:蘇我馬子]][[ko:소가노 우마코]][[nl:Soga no Umako]][[pl:Umako Soga]][[pt:Soga no Umako]][[ru:Сога-но Умако]][[th:โซงะ โนะ อูมาโกะ]][[tr:Soga no Umako]][[uk:Соґа но Умако]][[vi:Soga no Umako]][[zh-classical:蘇我馬子]][[zh-min-nan:Soga no Umako]][[zh-yue:蘇我馬子]][[zh:蘇我馬子]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1191233]] Wikidata name: Soga no Umako [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{family name hatnote|Soga|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | honorific_prefix = | name = Soga no Umako | honorific_suffix = | native_name = 蘇我馬子 | native_name_lang = jpn | image =File:Soga no Umako.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = | years_active = late 6th century – early 7th century | known_for = Political reforms of [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], associate of [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]], promoter of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] | birth_name = | birth_date = 0551 | birth_place = | death_date = {{death date|0626|6|19}} | death_place ={{ill|Asuka|WD=Q752397}} | death_cause = | resting_place = {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|WD=Q3816159}} | resting_place_coordinates = {{Coord|34|28|0.7|N|135|49|34.1|E|display=title}} | monuments = [[Ishibutai Kofun]] (traditionally) | other_names = {{Nihongo|Shima no Ōomi|嶋大臣}} | spouse = {{ill|Futohime|WD=Q11443857}}; {{ill|Kamaashi-hime|WD=Q106118985}} | partner = | children = {{ill|Kawakami no Iratsume|WD=Q11553322}}; {{ill|Q106095528|WD=Q106095528}}; {{ill|Soga no Emishi|WD=Q1292368}}; {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|WD=Q11625195}}; {{ill|Soga no Zentoko|WD=Q11625196}}; {{ill|Tojiko no Iratsume|WD=Q11396062}} | parents = [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] | relatives ={{ill|Oane-no-kimi|WD=Q11625199}}; {{ill|Sakaibe no Marise|WD=Q7402774}}; {{ill|Soga no Kitashihime|WD=Q7554458}} | gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}} | nationality = {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}} | occupation = {{ill|politician|WD=Q82955}} | title = {{ill|Ōomi|WD=Q4328809}} | father = {{ill|Soga no Iname|WD=Q595897}} | family = {{ill|Soga clan|WD=Q909854}} | notable_works = {{ill|Kokki|WD=Q826177}}; {{ill|Omi Muraji Tomo no Miyatsuko Kuni no Miyatsuko Momo Amari Yaso Tomonoo Awasete Ōmi Takara Domo no Moto tsu Fumi|WD=Q11612432}}; {{ill|Tennōki|WD=Q6141029}} }} [[File:IshibutaiBlueStairsScan3.jpg|thumb|right|[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is considered likely to have been intended as the tomb of Soga no Umako.]] {{nihongo|'''Soga no Umako'''|蘇我 馬子||551? – June 19, 626<ref>June 19, 626 corresponds to the twentieth day of the fifth month of 626 ([[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Heibo]]) of the traditional [[:en:en:Lunisolar calendar|en:Lunisolar calendar]] used in Japan until 1873</ref>}} was the son of [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] and a member of the powerful [[Soga clan]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
Umako conducted political reforms with [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] during the rules of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] and [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]]<ref>{{cite book |author=Mulhern, Chieko Irie |title=Heroic with grace: legendary women of Japan |publisher=M.E. Sharpe |location=Armonk, N.Y |year=1991 |page=40 |isbn=0-87332-552-4 }}</ref> and established the Soga clan's stronghold in the government by having his daughters married to members of the imperial family.
In the late 6th century, Soga no Umako went to great lengths to promote Buddhism in Japan, and was instrumental in its acceptance. At that time, the Soga clan employed immigrants from China and Korea, and worked to obtain advanced technology and other knowledge. In 587, Umako defeated [[:en:Mononobe no Moriya|Mononobe no Moriya]] in the [[:en:Soga–Mononobe conflict|Battle of Shigisan]], securing Soga dominance. On January 15, 593, relics of Buddha Shakyamuni Tathāgata were deposited inside the foundation stone under the pillar of a pagoda at [[:en:Asuka-dera|Asuka-dera]] (Hōkō-ji at the time), a temple whose construction Umako ordered, according to the Suiko section of the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]].<ref>{{cite book |author=Aston, W. G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }}</ref>
[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}}</ref>
==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children.
*[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[:en:Emperor Sushun|Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Shotoku Taishi|Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Emperor Jomei|Emperor Jomei]]
==References== <references/>]]{{Japan-bio-stub}}

}}

{{ill|Soga no Zentoko|WD=Q11625196}}; {{ill|Tojiko no Iratsume|WD=Q11396062}} | parents = [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] | relatives ={{ill|Oane-no-kimi|WD=Q11625199}}; {{ill|Sakaibe no Marise|WD=Q7402774}}; {{ill|Soga no Kitashihime|WD=Q7554458}} | gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}} | nationality = {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}} | occupation = {{ill|politician|WD=Q82955}} | title = {{ill|Ōomi|WD=Q4328809}} | father = {{ill|Soga no Iname|WD=Q595897}} | family = {{ill|Soga clan|WD=Q909854}} | notable_works = {{ill|Kokki|WD=Q826177}}; {{ill|Omi Muraji Tomo no Miyatsuko Kuni no Miyatsuko Momo Amari Yaso Tomonoo Awasete Ōmi Takara Domo no Moto tsu Fumi|WD=Q11612432}}; {{ill|Tennōki|WD=Q6141029}} }} [[File:IshibutaiBlueStairsScan3.jpg|thumb|right|[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is considered likely to have been intended as the tomb of Soga no Umako.]] {{nihongo|'''Soga no Umako'''|蘇我 馬子||551? – June 19, 626<ref>June 19, 626 corresponds to the twentieth day of the fifth month of 626 ([[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Heibo]]) of the traditional [[:en:en:Lunisolar calendar|en:Lunisolar calendar]] used in Japan until 1873</ref>}} was the son of [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] and a member of the powerful [[Soga clan]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
Umako conducted political reforms with [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] during the rules of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] and [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]]<ref>{{cite book |author=Mulhern, Chieko Irie |title=Heroic with grace: legendary women of Japan |publisher=M.E. Sharpe |location=Armonk, N.Y |year=1991 |page=40 |isbn=0-87332-552-4 }}</ref> and established the Soga clan's stronghold in the government by having his daughters married to members of the imperial family.
In the late 6th century, Soga no Umako went to great lengths to promote Buddhism in Japan, and was instrumental in its acceptance. At that time, the Soga clan employed immigrants from China and Korea, and worked to obtain advanced technology and other knowledge. In 587, Umako defeated [[:en:Mononobe no Moriya|Mononobe no Moriya]] in the [[:en:Soga–Mononobe conflict|Battle of Shigisan]], securing Soga dominance. On January 15, 593, relics of Buddha Shakyamuni Tathāgata were deposited inside the foundation stone under the pillar of a pagoda at [[:en:Asuka-dera|Asuka-dera]] (Hōkō-ji at the time), a temple whose construction Umako ordered, according to the Suiko section of the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]].<ref>{{cite book |author=Aston, W. G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }}</ref>
[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}}</ref>
==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children.
*[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[:en:Emperor Sushun|Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Shotoku Taishi|Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Emperor Jomei|Emperor Jomei]]
==References== <references/>]]{{Japan-bio-stub}}

{{ill|Kamaashi-hime|WD=Q106118985}} | partner = | children = {{ill|Kawakami no Iratsume|WD=Q11553322}}; {{ill|Q106095528|WD=Q106095528}}; {{ill|Soga no Emishi|WD=Q1292368}}; {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|WD=Q11625195}}; {{ill|Soga no Zentoko|WD=Q11625196}}; {{ill|Tojiko no Iratsume|WD=Q11396062}} | parents = [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] | relatives ={{ill|Oane-no-kimi|WD=Q11625199}}; {{ill|Sakaibe no Marise|WD=Q7402774}}; {{ill|Soga no Kitashihime|WD=Q7554458}} | gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}} | nationality = {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}} | occupation = {{ill|politician|WD=Q82955}} | title = {{ill|Ōomi|WD=Q4328809}} | father = {{ill|Soga no Iname|WD=Q595897}} | family = {{ill|Soga clan|WD=Q909854}} | notable_works = {{ill|Kokki|WD=Q826177}}; {{ill|Omi Muraji Tomo no Miyatsuko Kuni no Miyatsuko Momo Amari Yaso Tomonoo Awasete Ōmi Takara Domo no Moto tsu Fumi|WD=Q11612432}}; {{ill|Tennōki|WD=Q6141029}} }} [[File:IshibutaiBlueStairsScan3.jpg|thumb|right|[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is considered likely to have been intended as the tomb of Soga no Umako.]] {{nihongo|'''Soga no Umako'''|蘇我 馬子||551? – June 19, 626<ref>June 19, 626 corresponds to the twentieth day of the fifth month of 626 ([[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Heibo]]) of the traditional [[:en:en:Lunisolar calendar|en:Lunisolar calendar]] used in Japan until 1873</ref>}} was the son of [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] and a member of the powerful [[Soga clan]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
Umako conducted political reforms with [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] during the rules of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] and [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]]<ref>{{cite book |author=Mulhern, Chieko Irie |title=Heroic with grace: legendary women of Japan |publisher=M.E. Sharpe |location=Armonk, N.Y |year=1991 |page=40 |isbn=0-87332-552-4 }}</ref> and established the Soga clan's stronghold in the government by having his daughters married to members of the imperial family.
In the late 6th century, Soga no Umako went to great lengths to promote Buddhism in Japan, and was instrumental in its acceptance. At that time, the Soga clan employed immigrants from China and Korea, and worked to obtain advanced technology and other knowledge. In 587, Umako defeated [[:en:Mononobe no Moriya|Mononobe no Moriya]] in the [[:en:Soga–Mononobe conflict|Battle of Shigisan]], securing Soga dominance. On January 15, 593, relics of Buddha Shakyamuni Tathāgata were deposited inside the foundation stone under the pillar of a pagoda at [[:en:Asuka-dera|Asuka-dera]] (Hōkō-ji at the time), a temple whose construction Umako ordered, according to the Suiko section of the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]].<ref>{{cite book |author=Aston, W. G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }}</ref>
[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}}</ref>
==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children.
*[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[:en:Emperor Sushun|Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Shotoku Taishi|Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Emperor Jomei|Emperor Jomei]]
==References== <references/>]]{{Japan-bio-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:سوغا نو أوماكو]][[bn:সোগা নো উমাকো]][[ca:Soga no Umako]][[de:Soga no Umako]][[en:Soga no Umako]][[es:Soga no Umako]][[fa:سوگا نو اوماکو]][[fr:Soga no Umako]][[it:Soga no Umako]][[ja:蘇我馬子]][[ko:소가노 우마코]][[nl:Soga no Umako]][[pl:Umako Soga]][[pt:Soga no Umako]][[ru:Сога-но Умако]][[th:โซงะ โนะ อูมาโกะ]][[tr:Soga no Umako]][[uk:Соґа но Умако]][[vi:Soga no Umako]][[zh-classical:蘇我馬子]][[zh-min-nan:Soga no Umako]][[zh-yue:蘇我馬子]][[zh:蘇我馬子]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q1191233]] Wikidata name: Soga no Umako [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:31, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{family name hatnote|Soga|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | honorific_prefix = | name = Soga no Umako | honorific_suffix = | native_name = 蘇我馬子 | native_name_lang = jpn | image =File:Soga no Umako.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = | years_active = late 6th century – early 7th century | known_for = Political reforms of [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], associate of [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]], promoter of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] | birth_name = | birth_date = 0551 | birth_place = | death_date = {{death date|0626|6|19}} | death_place ={{ill|Asuka|WD=Q752397}} | death_cause = | resting_place = {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|WD=Q3816159}} | resting_place_coordinates = {{Coord|34|28|0.7|N|135|49|34.1|E|display=title}} | monuments = [[Ishibutai Kofun]] (traditionally) | other_names = {{Nihongo|Shima no Ōomi|嶋大臣}} | spouse = {{ill|Futohime|WD=Q11443857}}; {{ill|Kamaashi-hime|WD=Q106118985}} | partner = | children = {{ill|Kawakami no Iratsume|WD=Q11553322}}; {{ill|Q106095528|WD=Q106095528}}; {{ill|Soga no Emishi|WD=Q1292368}}; {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|WD=Q11625195}}; {{ill|Soga no Zentoko|WD=Q11625196}}; {{ill|Tojiko no Iratsume|WD=Q11396062}} | parents = [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] | relatives ={{ill|Oane-no-kimi|WD=Q11625199}}; {{ill|Sakaibe no Marise|WD=Q7402774}}; {{ill|Soga no Kitashihime|WD=Q7554458}} | gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}} | nationality = {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}} | occupation = {{ill|politician|WD=Q82955}} | title = {{ill|Ōomi|WD=Q4328809}} | father = {{ill|Soga no Iname|WD=Q595897}} | family = {{ill|Soga clan|WD=Q909854}} | notable_works = {{ill|Kokki|WD=Q826177}}; {{ill|Omi Muraji Tomo no Miyatsuko Kuni no Miyatsuko Momo Amari Yaso Tomonoo Awasete Ōmi Takara Domo no Moto tsu Fumi|WD=Q11612432}}; {{ill|Tennōki|WD=Q6141029}} }} [[File:IshibutaiBlueStairsScan3.jpg|thumb|right|[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is considered likely to have been intended as the tomb of Soga no Umako.]] {{nihongo|'''Soga no Umako'''|蘇我 馬子||551? – June 19, 626<ref>June 19, 626 corresponds to the twentieth day of the fifth month of 626 ([[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Heibo]]) of the traditional [[:en:en:Lunisolar calendar|en:Lunisolar calendar]] used in Japan until 1873</ref>}} was the son of [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] and a member of the powerful [[Soga clan]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
Umako conducted political reforms with [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] during the rules of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] and [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]]<ref>{{cite book |author=Mulhern, Chieko Irie |title=Heroic with grace: legendary women of Japan |publisher=M.E. Sharpe |location=Armonk, N.Y |year=1991 |page=40 |isbn=0-87332-552-4 }}</ref> and established the Soga clan's stronghold in the government by having his daughters married to members of the imperial family.
In the late 6th century, Soga no Umako went to great lengths to promote Buddhism in Japan, and was instrumental in its acceptance. At that time, the Soga clan employed immigrants from China and Korea, and worked to obtain advanced technology and other knowledge. In 587, Umako defeated [[:en:Mononobe no Moriya|Mononobe no Moriya]] in the [[:en:Soga–Mononobe conflict|Battle of Shigisan]], securing Soga dominance. On January 15, 593, relics of Buddha Shakyamuni Tathāgata were deposited inside the foundation stone under the pillar of a pagoda at [[:en:Asuka-dera|Asuka-dera]] (Hōkō-ji at the time), a temple whose construction Umako ordered, according to the Suiko section of the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]].<ref>{{cite book |author=Aston, W. G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }}</ref>
[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}}</ref>
==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children.
*[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[:en:Emperor Sushun|Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Shotoku Taishi|Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Emperor Jomei|Emperor Jomei]]
==References== <references/>]]{{Japan-bio-stub}}

{{ill|Kamaashi-hime|WD=Q106118985}} | partner = | children = {{ill|Kawakami no Iratsume|WD=Q11553322}}; {{ill|Q106095528|WD=Q106095528}}; {{ill|Soga no Emishi|WD=Q1292368}}; {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|WD=Q11625195}}; {{ill|Soga no Zentoko|WD=Q11625196}}; {{ill|Tojiko no Iratsume|WD=Q11396062}} | parents = [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] | relatives ={{ill|Oane-no-kimi|WD=Q11625199}}; {{ill|Sakaibe no Marise|WD=Q7402774}}; {{ill|Soga no Kitashihime|WD=Q7554458}} | gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}} | nationality = {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}} | occupation = {{ill|politician|WD=Q82955}} | title = {{ill|Ōomi|WD=Q4328809}} | father = {{ill|Soga no Iname|WD=Q595897}} | family = {{ill|Soga clan|WD=Q909854}} | notable_works = {{ill|Kokki|WD=Q826177}}; {{ill|Omi Muraji Tomo no Miyatsuko Kuni no Miyatsuko Momo Amari Yaso Tomonoo Awasete Ōmi Takara Domo no Moto tsu Fumi|WD=Q11612432}}; {{ill|Tennōki|WD=Q6141029}} }} [[File:IshibutaiBlueStairsScan3.jpg|thumb|right|[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is considered likely to have been intended as the tomb of Soga no Umako.]] {{nihongo|'''Soga no Umako'''|蘇我 馬子||551? – June 19, 626<ref>June 19, 626 corresponds to the twentieth day of the fifth month of 626 ([[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Heibo]]) of the traditional [[:en:en:Lunisolar calendar|en:Lunisolar calendar]] used in Japan until 1873</ref>}} was the son of [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] and a member of the powerful [[Soga clan]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
Umako conducted political reforms with [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] during the rules of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] and [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]]<ref>{{cite book |author=Mulhern, Chieko Irie |title=Heroic with grace: legendary women of Japan |publisher=M.E. Sharpe |location=Armonk, N.Y |year=1991 |page=40 |isbn=0-87332-552-4 }}</ref> and established the Soga clan's stronghold in the government by having his daughters married to members of the imperial family.
In the late 6th century, Soga no Umako went to great lengths to promote Buddhism in Japan, and was instrumental in its acceptance. At that time, the Soga clan employed immigrants from China and Korea, and worked to obtain advanced technology and other knowledge. In 587, Umako defeated [[:en:Mononobe no Moriya|Mononobe no Moriya]] in the [[:en:Soga–Mononobe conflict|Battle of Shigisan]], securing Soga dominance. On January 15, 593, relics of Buddha Shakyamuni Tathāgata were deposited inside the foundation stone under the pillar of a pagoda at [[:en:Asuka-dera|Asuka-dera]] (Hōkō-ji at the time), a temple whose construction Umako ordered, according to the Suiko section of the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]].<ref>{{cite book |author=Aston, W. G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }}</ref>
[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}}</ref>
==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children.
*[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[:en:Emperor Sushun|Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Shotoku Taishi|Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Emperor Jomei|Emperor Jomei]]
==References== <references/>]]{{Japan-bio-stub}}

Political reforms of [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], associate of [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]], promoter of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] | birth_name = | birth_date = 0551 | birth_place = | death_date = {{death date|0626|6|19}} | death_place ={{ill|Asuka|WD=Q752397}} | death_cause = | resting_place = {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|WD=Q3816159}} | resting_place_coordinates = {{Coord|34|28|0.7|N|135|49|34.1|E|display=title}} | monuments = [[Ishibutai Kofun]] (traditionally) | other_names = {{Nihongo|Shima no Ōomi|嶋大臣}} | spouse = {{ill|Futohime|WD=Q11443857}}; {{ill|Kamaashi-hime|WD=Q106118985}} | partner = | children = {{ill|Kawakami no Iratsume|WD=Q11553322}}; {{ill|Q106095528|WD=Q106095528}}; {{ill|Soga no Emishi|WD=Q1292368}}; {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|WD=Q11625195}}; {{ill|Soga no Zentoko|WD=Q11625196}}; {{ill|Tojiko no Iratsume|WD=Q11396062}} | parents = [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] | relatives ={{ill|Oane-no-kimi|WD=Q11625199}}; {{ill|Sakaibe no Marise|WD=Q7402774}}; {{ill|Soga no Kitashihime|WD=Q7554458}} | gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}} | nationality = {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}} | occupation = {{ill|politician|WD=Q82955}} | title = {{ill|Ōomi|WD=Q4328809}} | father = {{ill|Soga no Iname|WD=Q595897}} | family = {{ill|Soga clan|WD=Q909854}} | notable_works = {{ill|Kokki|WD=Q826177}}; {{ill|Omi Muraji Tomo no Miyatsuko Kuni no Miyatsuko Momo Amari Yaso Tomonoo Awasete Ōmi Takara Domo no Moto tsu Fumi|WD=Q11612432}}; {{ill|Tennōki|WD=Q6141029}} }} [[File:IshibutaiBlueStairsScan3.jpg|thumb|right|[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is considered likely to have been intended as the tomb of Soga no Umako.]] {{nihongo|'''Soga no Umako'''|蘇我 馬子||551? – June 19, 626<ref>June 19, 626 corresponds to the twentieth day of the fifth month of 626 ([[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Heibo]]) of the traditional [[:en:en:Lunisolar calendar|en:Lunisolar calendar]] used in Japan until 1873</ref>}} was the son of [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] and a member of the powerful [[Soga clan]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
Umako conducted political reforms with [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] during the rules of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] and [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]]<ref>{{cite book |author=Mulhern, Chieko Irie |title=Heroic with grace: legendary women of Japan |publisher=M.E. Sharpe |location=Armonk, N.Y |year=1991 |page=40 |isbn=0-87332-552-4 }}</ref> and established the Soga clan's stronghold in the government by having his daughters married to members of the imperial family.
In the late 6th century, Soga no Umako went to great lengths to promote Buddhism in Japan, and was instrumental in its acceptance. At that time, the Soga clan employed immigrants from China and Korea, and worked to obtain advanced technology and other knowledge. In 587, Umako defeated [[:en:Mononobe no Moriya|Mononobe no Moriya]] in the [[:en:Soga–Mononobe conflict|Battle of Shigisan]], securing Soga dominance. On January 15, 593, relics of Buddha Shakyamuni Tathāgata were deposited inside the foundation stone under the pillar of a pagoda at [[:en:Asuka-dera|Asuka-dera]] (Hōkō-ji at the time), a temple whose construction Umako ordered, according to the Suiko section of the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]].<ref>{{cite book |author=Aston, W. G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }}</ref>
[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}}</ref>
==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children.
*[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[:en:Emperor Sushun|Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Shotoku Taishi|Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Emperor Jomei|Emperor Jomei]]
==References== <references/>]]{{Japan-bio-stub}}

{{ill|Sakaibe no Marise|WD=Q7402774}}; {{ill|Soga no Kitashihime|WD=Q7554458}} | gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}} | nationality = {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}} | occupation = {{ill|politician|WD=Q82955}} | title = {{ill|Ōomi|WD=Q4328809}} | father = {{ill|Soga no Iname|WD=Q595897}} | family = {{ill|Soga clan|WD=Q909854}} | notable_works = {{ill|Kokki|WD=Q826177}}; {{ill|Omi Muraji Tomo no Miyatsuko Kuni no Miyatsuko Momo Amari Yaso Tomonoo Awasete Ōmi Takara Domo no Moto tsu Fumi|WD=Q11612432}}; {{ill|Tennōki|WD=Q6141029}} }} [[File:IshibutaiBlueStairsScan3.jpg|thumb|right|[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is considered likely to have been intended as the tomb of Soga no Umako.]] {{nihongo|'''Soga no Umako'''|蘇我 馬子||551? – June 19, 626<ref>June 19, 626 corresponds to the twentieth day of the fifth month of 626 ([[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Heibo]]) of the traditional [[:en:en:Lunisolar calendar|en:Lunisolar calendar]] used in Japan until 1873</ref>}} was the son of [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] and a member of the powerful [[Soga clan]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
Umako conducted political reforms with [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] during the rules of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] and [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]]<ref>{{cite book |author=Mulhern, Chieko Irie |title=Heroic with grace: legendary women of Japan |publisher=M.E. Sharpe |location=Armonk, N.Y |year=1991 |page=40 |isbn=0-87332-552-4 }}</ref> and established the Soga clan's stronghold in the government by having his daughters married to members of the imperial family.
In the late 6th century, Soga no Umako went to great lengths to promote Buddhism in Japan, and was instrumental in its acceptance. At that time, the Soga clan employed immigrants from China and Korea, and worked to obtain advanced technology and other knowledge. In 587, Umako defeated [[:en:Mononobe no Moriya|Mononobe no Moriya]] in the [[:en:Soga–Mononobe conflict|Battle of Shigisan]], securing Soga dominance. On January 15, 593, relics of Buddha Shakyamuni Tathāgata were deposited inside the foundation stone under the pillar of a pagoda at [[:en:Asuka-dera|Asuka-dera]] (Hōkō-ji at the time), a temple whose construction Umako ordered, according to the Suiko section of the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]].<ref>{{cite book |author=Aston, W. G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }}</ref>
[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}}</ref>
==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children.
*[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[:en:Emperor Sushun|Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Shotoku Taishi|Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Emperor Jomei|Emperor Jomei]]
==References== <references/>]]{{Japan-bio-stub}}
