Political reforms of [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], associate of [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]], promoter of [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] | birth_name = | birth_date = 0551 | birth_place = | death_date = {{death date|0626|6|19}} | death_place ={{ill|Asuka|WD=Q752397}} | death_cause = | resting_place = {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|WD=Q3816159}} | resting_place_coordinates = {{Coord|34|28|0.7|N|135|49|34.1|E|display=title}} | monuments = [[Ishibutai Kofun]] (traditionally) | other_names = {{Nihongo|Shima no Ōomi|嶋大臣}} | spouse = {{ill|Futohime|WD=Q11443857}}; {{ill|Kamaashi-hime|WD=Q106118985}} | partner = | children = {{ill|Kawakami no Iratsume|WD=Q11553322}}; {{ill|Q106095528|WD=Q106095528}}; {{ill|Soga no Emishi|WD=Q1292368}}; {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|WD=Q11625195}}; {{ill|Soga no Zentoko|WD=Q11625196}}; {{ill|Tojiko no Iratsume|WD=Q11396062}} | parents = [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] | relatives ={{ill|Oane-no-kimi|WD=Q11625199}}; {{ill|Sakaibe no Marise|WD=Q7402774}}; {{ill|Soga no Kitashihime|WD=Q7554458}} | gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}} | nationality = {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}} | occupation = {{ill|politician|WD=Q82955}} | title = {{ill|Ōomi|WD=Q4328809}} | father = {{ill|Soga no Iname|WD=Q595897}} | family = {{ill|Soga clan|WD=Q909854}} | notable_works = {{ill|Kokki|WD=Q826177}}; {{ill|Omi Muraji Tomo no Miyatsuko Kuni no Miyatsuko Momo Amari Yaso Tomonoo Awasete Ōmi Takara Domo no Moto tsu Fumi|WD=Q11612432}}; {{ill|Tennōki|WD=Q6141029}} }} [[File:IshibutaiBlueStairsScan3.jpg|thumb|right|[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is considered likely to have been intended as the tomb of Soga no Umako.]] {{nihongo|'''Soga no Umako'''|蘇我 馬子||551? – June 19, 626<ref>June 19, 626 corresponds to the twentieth day of the fifth month of 626 ([[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Heibo]]) of the traditional [[:en:en:Lunisolar calendar|en:Lunisolar calendar]] used in Japan until 1873</ref>}} was the son of [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] and a member of the powerful [[Soga clan]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
Umako conducted political reforms with [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] during the rules of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] and [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]]<ref>{{cite book |author=Mulhern, Chieko Irie |title=Heroic with grace: legendary women of Japan |publisher=M.E. Sharpe |location=Armonk, N.Y |year=1991 |page=40 |isbn=0-87332-552-4 }}</ref> and established the Soga clan's stronghold in the government by having his daughters married to members of the imperial family.
In the late 6th century, Soga no Umako went to great lengths to promote Buddhism in Japan, and was instrumental in its acceptance. At that time, the Soga clan employed immigrants from China and Korea, and worked to obtain advanced technology and other knowledge. In 587, Umako defeated [[:en:Mononobe no Moriya|Mononobe no Moriya]] in the [[:en:Soga–Mononobe conflict|Battle of Shigisan]], securing Soga dominance. On January 15, 593, relics of Buddha Shakyamuni Tathāgata were deposited inside the foundation stone under the pillar of a pagoda at [[:en:Asuka-dera|Asuka-dera]] (Hōkō-ji at the time), a temple whose construction Umako ordered, according to the Suiko section of the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]].<ref>{{cite book |author=Aston, W. G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }}</ref>
[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}}</ref>
==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children.
*[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[:en:Emperor Sushun|Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Shotoku Taishi|Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Emperor Jomei|Emperor Jomei]]
==References== <references/>]]{{Japan-bio-stub}}

member of the powerful [[Soga clan]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
Umako conducted political reforms with [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] during the rules of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] and [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]]<ref>{{cite book |author=Mulhern, Chieko Irie |title=Heroic with grace: legendary women of Japan |publisher=M.E. Sharpe |location=Armonk, N.Y |year=1991 |page=40 |isbn=0-87332-552-4 }}</ref> and established the Soga clan's stronghold in the government by having his daughters married to members of the imperial family.
In the late 6th century, Soga no Umako went to great lengths to promote Buddhism in Japan, and was instrumental in its acceptance. At that time, the Soga clan employed immigrants from China and Korea, and worked to obtain advanced technology and other knowledge. In 587, Umako defeated [[:en:Mononobe no Moriya|Mononobe no Moriya]] in the [[:en:Soga–Mononobe conflict|Battle of Shigisan]], securing Soga dominance. On January 15, 593, relics of Buddha Shakyamuni Tathāgata were deposited inside the foundation stone under the pillar of a pagoda at [[:en:Asuka-dera|Asuka-dera]] (Hōkō-ji at the time), a temple whose construction Umako ordered, according to the Suiko section of the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]].<ref>{{cite book |author=Aston, W. G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }}</ref>
[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}}</ref>
==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children.
*[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[:en:Emperor Sushun|Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Shotoku Taishi|Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Emperor Jomei|Emperor Jomei]]
==References== <references/>]]{{Japan-bio-stub}}

{{ill|Sakaibe no Marise|WD=Q7402774}}; {{ill|Soga no Kitashihime|WD=Q7554458}} | gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}} | nationality = {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}} | occupation = {{ill|politician|WD=Q82955}} | title = {{ill|Ōomi|WD=Q4328809}} | father = {{ill|Soga no Iname|WD=Q595897}} | family = {{ill|Soga clan|WD=Q909854}} | notable_works = {{ill|Kokki|WD=Q826177}}; {{ill|Omi Muraji Tomo no Miyatsuko Kuni no Miyatsuko Momo Amari Yaso Tomonoo Awasete Ōmi Takara Domo no Moto tsu Fumi|WD=Q11612432}}; {{ill|Tennōki|WD=Q6141029}} }} [[File:IshibutaiBlueStairsScan3.jpg|thumb|right|[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is considered likely to have been intended as the tomb of Soga no Umako.]] {{nihongo|'''Soga no Umako'''|蘇我 馬子||551? – June 19, 626<ref>June 19, 626 corresponds to the twentieth day of the fifth month of 626 ([[:en:Sexagenary cycle|Heibo]]) of the traditional [[:en:en:Lunisolar calendar|en:Lunisolar calendar]] used in Japan until 1873</ref>}} was the son of [[:en:Soga no Iname|Soga no Iname]] and a member of the powerful [[Soga clan]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]].
Umako conducted political reforms with [[:en:Prince Shōtoku|Prince Shōtoku]] during the rules of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] and [[:en:Empress Suiko|Empress Suiko]]<ref>{{cite book |author=Mulhern, Chieko Irie |title=Heroic with grace: legendary women of Japan |publisher=M.E. Sharpe |location=Armonk, N.Y |year=1991 |page=40 |isbn=0-87332-552-4 }}</ref> and established the Soga clan's stronghold in the government by having his daughters married to members of the imperial family.
In the late 6th century, Soga no Umako went to great lengths to promote Buddhism in Japan, and was instrumental in its acceptance. At that time, the Soga clan employed immigrants from China and Korea, and worked to obtain advanced technology and other knowledge. In 587, Umako defeated [[:en:Mononobe no Moriya|Mononobe no Moriya]] in the [[:en:Soga–Mononobe conflict|Battle of Shigisan]], securing Soga dominance. On January 15, 593, relics of Buddha Shakyamuni Tathāgata were deposited inside the foundation stone under the pillar of a pagoda at [[:en:Asuka-dera|Asuka-dera]] (Hōkō-ji at the time), a temple whose construction Umako ordered, according to the Suiko section of the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]].<ref>{{cite book |author=Aston, W. G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }}</ref>
[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}}</ref>
==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children.
*[[:en:Soga no Emishi|Soga no Emishi]] *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of [[:en:Emperor Sushun|Emperor Sushun]] in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Shotoku Taishi|Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Emperor Jomei|Emperor Jomei]]
==References== <references/>]]{{Japan-bio-stub}}

{{nihongo|'''Soga no Umako'''|蘇我 馬子||551? – June 19, 626<ref>June 19, 626 corresponds to the twentieth day of the fifth month of 626 ({{ill|Sexagenary cycle|en|Sexagenary cycle|WD=Q246658|lt=Heibo}}) of the traditional {{ill|en:Lunisolar calendar|en|en:Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=en:Lunisolar calendar}} used in Japan until 1873</ref>}} was the son of {{ill|Soga no Iname|en|Soga no Iname|WD=Q595897|lt=Soga no Iname}} and a member of the powerful [[Soga clan]] of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}.
Umako conducted political reforms with {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku}} during the rules of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] and {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko}}<ref>{{cite book |author=Mulhern, Chieko Irie |title=Heroic with grace: legendary women of Japan |publisher=M.E. Sharpe |location=Armonk, N.Y |year=1991 |page=40 |isbn=0-87332-552-4 }}</ref> and established the Soga clan's stronghold in the government by having his daughters married to members of the imperial family.
In the late 6th century, Soga no Umako went to great lengths to promote Buddhism in Japan, and was instrumental in its acceptance. At that time, the Soga clan employed immigrants from China and Korea, and worked to obtain advanced technology and other knowledge. In 587, Umako defeated {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|en|Mononobe no Moriya|WD=Q2424736|lt=Mononobe no Moriya}} in the {{ill|Soga–Mononobe conflict|en|Soga–Mononobe conflict|WD=Q534720|lt=Battle of Shigisan}}, securing Soga dominance. On January 15, 593, relics of Buddha Shakyamuni Tathāgata were deposited inside the foundation stone under the pillar of a pagoda at {{ill|Asuka-dera|en|Asuka-dera|WD=Q779755|lt=Asuka-dera}} (Hōkō-ji at the time), a temple whose construction Umako ordered, according to the Suiko section of the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]].<ref>{{cite book |author=Aston, W. G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }}</ref>
[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}}</ref>
==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children.

of the traditional {{ill|en:Lunisolar calendar|en|en:Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=en:Lunisolar calendar}} used in Japan until 1873</ref>}} was the son of {{ill|Soga no Iname|en|Soga no Iname|WD=Q595897|lt=Soga no Iname}} and a member of the powerful [[Soga clan]] of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}.
Umako conducted political reforms with {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku}} during the rules of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] and {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko}}<ref>{{cite book |author=Mulhern, Chieko Irie |title=Heroic with grace: legendary women of Japan |publisher=M.E. Sharpe |location=Armonk, N.Y |year=1991 |page=40 |isbn=0-87332-552-4 }}</ref> and established the Soga clan's stronghold in the government by having his daughters married to members of the imperial family.
In the late 6th century, Soga no Umako went to great lengths to promote Buddhism in Japan, and was instrumental in its acceptance. At that time, the Soga clan employed immigrants from China and Korea, and worked to obtain advanced technology and other knowledge. In 587, Umako defeated {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|en|Mononobe no Moriya|WD=Q2424736|lt=Mononobe no Moriya}} in the {{ill|Soga–Mononobe conflict|en|Soga–Mononobe conflict|WD=Q534720|lt=Battle of Shigisan}}, securing Soga dominance. On January 15, 593, relics of Buddha Shakyamuni Tathāgata were deposited inside the foundation stone under the pillar of a pagoda at {{ill|Asuka-dera|en|Asuka-dera|WD=Q779755|lt=Asuka-dera}} (Hōkō-ji at the time), a temple whose construction Umako ordered, according to the Suiko section of the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]].<ref>{{cite book |author=Aston, W. G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }}</ref>
[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}}</ref>
==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children.
*{{ill|Soga no Emishi|en|Soga no Emishi|WD=Q1292368|lt=Soga no Emishi}} *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of {{ill|Emperor Sushun|en|Emperor Sushun|WD=Q349238|lt=Emperor Sushun}} in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Shotoku Taishi|Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of {{ill|Emperor Jomei|en|Emperor Jomei|WD=Q329761|lt=Emperor Jomei}}
==References== <references/>]]{{Japan-bio-stub}}

Fumi|WD=Q11612432}}; {{ill|Tennōki|WD=Q6141029}} }} [[File:IshibutaiBlueStairsScan3.jpg|thumb|right|[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is considered likely to have been intended as the tomb of Soga no Umako.]] {{nihongo|'''Soga no Umako'''|蘇我 馬子||551? – June 19, 626<ref>June 19, 626 corresponds to the twentieth day of the fifth month of 626 ({{ill|Sexagenary cycle|en|Sexagenary cycle|WD=Q246658|lt=Heibo}}) of the traditional {{ill|en:Lunisolar calendar|en|en:Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=en:Lunisolar calendar}} used in Japan until 1873</ref>}} was the son of {{ill|Soga no Iname|en|Soga no Iname|WD=Q595897|lt=Soga no Iname}} and a member of the powerful [[Soga clan]] of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}.
Umako conducted political reforms with {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku}} during the rules of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] and {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko}}<ref>{{cite book |author=Mulhern, Chieko Irie |title=Heroic with grace: legendary women of Japan |publisher=M.E. Sharpe |location=Armonk, N.Y |year=1991 |page=40 |isbn=0-87332-552-4 }}</ref> and established the Soga clan's stronghold in the government by having his daughters married to members of the imperial family.
In the late 6th century, Soga no Umako went to great lengths to promote Buddhism in Japan, and was instrumental in its acceptance. At that time, the Soga clan employed immigrants from China and Korea, and worked to obtain advanced technology and other knowledge. In 587, Umako defeated {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|en|Mononobe no Moriya|WD=Q2424736|lt=Mononobe no Moriya}} in the {{ill|Soga–Mononobe conflict|en|Soga–Mononobe conflict|WD=Q534720|lt=Battle of Shigisan}}, securing Soga dominance. On January 15, 593, relics of Buddha Shakyamuni Tathāgata were deposited inside the foundation stone under the pillar of a pagoda at {{ill|Asuka-dera|en|Asuka-dera|WD=Q779755|lt=Asuka-dera}} (Hōkō-ji at the time), a temple whose construction Umako ordered, according to the Suiko section of the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]].<ref>{{cite book |author=Aston, W. G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }}</ref>
[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}}</ref>
==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children.
*{{ill|Soga no Emishi|en|Soga no Emishi|WD=Q1292368|lt=Soga no Emishi}} *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of {{ill|Emperor Sushun|en|Emperor Sushun|WD=Q349238|lt=Emperor Sushun}} in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Shotoku Taishi|Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of {{ill|Emperor Jomei|en|Emperor Jomei|WD=Q329761|lt=Emperor Jomei}}
==References== <references/>]]{{Japan-bio-stub}}

{{family name hatnote|Soga|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox person | honorific_prefix = | name = Soga no Umako | honorific_suffix = | native_name = 蘇我馬子 | native_name_lang = jpn | image =File:Soga no Umako.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = | years_active = late 6th century – early 7th century | known_for = Political reforms of {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}}, associate of {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku}}, promoter of {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism}} | birth_name = | birth_date = 0551 | birth_place = | death_date = {{death date|0626|6|19}} | death_place ={{ill|Asuka|WD=Q752397}} | death_cause = | resting_place = {{ill|Ishibutai Kofun|WD=Q3816159}} | resting_place_coordinates = {{Coord|34|28|0.7|N|135|49|34.1|E|display=title}} | monuments = [[Ishibutai Kofun]] (traditionally) | other_names = {{Nihongo|Shima no Ōomi|嶋大臣}} | spouse = {{ill|Futohime|WD=Q11443857}}; {{ill|Kamaashi-hime|WD=Q106118985}} | partner = | children = {{ill|Kawakami no Iratsume|WD=Q11553322}}; {{ill|Q106095528|WD=Q106095528}}; {{ill|Soga no Emishi|WD=Q1292368}}; {{ill|Soga no Kuramaro|WD=Q11625195}}; {{ill|Soga no Zentoko|WD=Q11625196}}; {{ill|Tojiko no Iratsume|WD=Q11396062}} | parents = {{ill|Soga no Iname|en|Soga no Iname|WD=Q595897|lt=Soga no Iname}} | relatives ={{ill|Oane-no-kimi|WD=Q11625199}}; {{ill|Sakaibe no Marise|WD=Q7402774}}; {{ill|Soga no Kitashihime|WD=Q7554458}} | gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}} | nationality = {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}} | occupation = {{ill|politician|WD=Q82955}} | title = {{ill|Ōomi|WD=Q4328809}} | father = {{ill|Soga no Iname|WD=Q595897}} | family = {{ill|Soga clan|WD=Q909854}} | notable_works = {{ill|Kokki|WD=Q826177}}; {{ill|Omi Muraji Tomo no Miyatsuko Kuni no Miyatsuko Momo Amari Yaso Tomonoo Awasete Ōmi Takara Domo no Moto tsu Fumi|WD=Q11612432}}; {{ill|Tennōki|WD=Q6141029}} }} [[File:IshibutaiBlueStairsScan3.jpg|thumb|right|[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is considered likely to have been intended as the tomb of Soga no Umako.]] {{nihongo|'''Soga no Umako'''|蘇我 馬子||551? – June 19, 626<ref>June 19, 626 corresponds to the twentieth day of the fifth month of 626 ({{ill|Sexagenary cycle|en|Sexagenary cycle|WD=Q246658|lt=Heibo}}) of the traditional {{ill|en:Lunisolar calendar|en|en:Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=en:Lunisolar calendar}} used in Japan until 1873</ref>}} was the son of {{ill|Soga no Iname|en|Soga no Iname|WD=Q595897|lt=Soga no Iname}} and a member of the powerful [[Soga clan]] of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}.
Umako conducted political reforms with {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku}} during the rules of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] and {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko}}<ref>{{cite book |author=Mulhern, Chieko Irie |title=Heroic with grace: legendary women of Japan |publisher=M.E. Sharpe |location=Armonk, N.Y |year=1991 |page=40 |isbn=0-87332-552-4 }}</ref> and established the Soga clan's stronghold in the government by having his daughters married to members of the imperial family.
In the late 6th century, Soga no Umako went to great lengths to promote Buddhism in Japan, and was instrumental in its acceptance. At that time, the Soga clan employed immigrants from China and Korea, and worked to obtain advanced technology and other knowledge. In 587, Umako defeated {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|en|Mononobe no Moriya|WD=Q2424736|lt=Mononobe no Moriya}} in the {{ill|Soga–Mononobe conflict|en|Soga–Mononobe conflict|WD=Q534720|lt=Battle of Shigisan}}, securing Soga dominance. On January 15, 593, relics of Buddha Shakyamuni Tathāgata were deposited inside the foundation stone under the pillar of a pagoda at {{ill|Asuka-dera|en|Asuka-dera|WD=Q779755|lt=Asuka-dera}} (Hōkō-ji at the time), a temple whose construction Umako ordered, according to the Suiko section of the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]].<ref>{{cite book |author=Aston, W. G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }}</ref>
[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}}</ref>
==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children.
*{{ill|Soga no Emishi|en|Soga no Emishi|WD=Q1292368|lt=Soga no Emishi}} *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of {{ill|Emperor Sushun|en|Emperor Sushun|WD=Q349238|lt=Emperor Sushun}} in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Shotoku Taishi|Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of {{ill|Emperor Jomei|en|Emperor Jomei|WD=Q329761|lt=Emperor Jomei}}
==References== <references/>]]{{Japan-bio-stub}}

{{ill|Sakaibe no Marise|WD=Q7402774|1=Sakaibe no Marise}}; {{ill|Soga no Kitashihime|WD=Q7554458|1=Soga no Kitashihime}} | gender = {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}} | nationality = {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17|1=Japan}} | occupation = {{ill|politician|WD=Q82955|1=Politician}} | title = {{ill|Ōomi|WD=Q4328809|1=Ōomi}} | father = {{ill|Soga no Iname|WD=Q595897|1=Soga no Iname}} | family = {{ill|Soga clan|WD=Q909854|1=Soga clan}} | notable_works = {{ill|Kokki|WD=Q826177|1=Kokki}}; {{ill|Omi Muraji Tomo no Miyatsuko Kuni no Miyatsuko Momo Amari Yaso Tomonoo Awasete Ōmi Takara Domo no Moto tsu Fumi|WD=Q11612432|1=Omi Muraji Tomo no Miyatsuko Kuni no Miyatsuko Momo Amari Yaso Tomonoo Awasete Ōmi Takara Domo no Moto tsu Fumi}}; {{ill|Tennōki|WD=Q6141029|1=Tennōki}} }} [[File:IshibutaiBlueStairsScan3.jpg|thumb|right|[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is considered likely to have been intended as the tomb of Soga no Umako.]] {{nihongo|'''Soga no Umako'''|蘇我 馬子||551? – June 19, 626<ref>June 19, 626 corresponds to the twentieth day of the fifth month of 626 ({{ill|Sexagenary cycle|en|Sexagenary cycle|WD=Q246658|lt=Heibo|1=Sexagenary cycle}}) of the traditional {{ill|en:Lunisolar calendar|en|en:Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=en:Lunisolar calendar|1=Lunisolar calendar}} used in Japan until 1873</ref>}} was the son of {{ill|Soga no Iname|en|Soga no Iname|WD=Q595897|lt=Soga no Iname|1=Soga no Iname}} and a member of the powerful [[Soga clan]] of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}.
Umako conducted political reforms with {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}} during the rules of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] and {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}}<ref>{{cite book |author=Mulhern, Chieko Irie |title=Heroic with grace: legendary women of Japan |publisher=M.E. Sharpe |location=Armonk, N.Y |year=1991 |page=40 |isbn=0-87332-552-4 }}</ref> and established the Soga clan's stronghold in the government by having his daughters married to members of the imperial family.
In the late 6th century, Soga no Umako went to great lengths to promote Buddhism in Japan, and was instrumental in its acceptance. At that time, the Soga clan employed immigrants from China and Korea, and worked to obtain advanced technology and other knowledge. In 587, Umako defeated {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|en|Mononobe no Moriya|WD=Q2424736|lt=Mononobe no Moriya|1=Mononobe no Moriya}} in the {{ill|Soga–Mononobe conflict|en|Soga–Mononobe conflict|WD=Q534720|lt=Battle of Shigisan|1=Soga–Mononobe conflict}}, securing Soga dominance. On January 15, 593, relics of Buddha Shakyamuni Tathāgata were deposited inside the foundation stone under the pillar of a pagoda at {{ill|Asuka-dera|en|Asuka-dera|WD=Q779755|lt=Asuka-dera|1=Asuka-dera}} (Hōkō-ji at the time), a temple whose construction Umako ordered, according to the Suiko section of the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]].<ref>{{cite book |author=Aston, W. G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }}</ref>
[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}}</ref>
==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children.
*{{ill|Soga no Emishi|en|Soga no Emishi|WD=Q1292368|lt=Soga no Emishi|1=Soga no Emishi}} *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of {{ill|Emperor Sushun|en|Emperor Sushun|WD=Q349238|lt=Emperor Sushun|1=Emperor Sushun}} in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Shotoku Taishi|Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of {{ill|Emperor Jomei|en|Emperor Jomei|WD=Q329761|lt=Emperor Jomei|1=Emperor Jomei}}
==References== <references/>]]{{Japan-bio-stub}}

{{ill|Tennōki|WD=Q6141029|1=Tennōki}} }} [[File:IshibutaiBlueStairsScan3.jpg|thumb|right|[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is considered likely to have been intended as the tomb of Soga no Umako.]] {{nihongo|'''Soga no Umako'''|蘇我 馬子||551? – June 19, 626<ref>June 19, 626 corresponds to the twentieth day of the fifth month of 626 ({{ill|Sexagenary cycle|en|Sexagenary cycle|WD=Q246658|lt=Heibo|1=Sexagenary cycle}}) of the traditional {{ill|en:Lunisolar calendar|en|en:Lunisolar calendar|WD=Q194235|lt=en:Lunisolar calendar|1=Lunisolar calendar}} used in Japan until 1873</ref>}} was the son of {{ill|Soga no Iname|en|Soga no Iname|WD=Q595897|lt=Soga no Iname|1=Soga no Iname}} and a member of the powerful [[Soga clan]] of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}.
Umako conducted political reforms with {{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|WD=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}} during the rules of [[Emperor Bidatsu]] and {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|WD=Q298057|lt=Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko}}<ref>{{cite book |author=Mulhern, Chieko Irie |title=Heroic with grace: legendary women of Japan |publisher=M.E. Sharpe |location=Armonk, N.Y |year=1991 |page=40 |isbn=0-87332-552-4 }}</ref> and established the Soga clan's stronghold in the government by having his daughters married to members of the imperial family.
In the late 6th century, Soga no Umako went to great lengths to promote Buddhism in Japan, and was instrumental in its acceptance. At that time, the Soga clan employed immigrants from China and Korea, and worked to obtain advanced technology and other knowledge. In 587, Umako defeated {{ill|Mononobe no Moriya|en|Mononobe no Moriya|WD=Q2424736|lt=Mononobe no Moriya|1=Mononobe no Moriya}} in the {{ill|Soga–Mononobe conflict|en|Soga–Mononobe conflict|WD=Q534720|lt=Battle of Shigisan|1=Soga–Mononobe conflict}}, securing Soga dominance. On January 15, 593, relics of Buddha Shakyamuni Tathāgata were deposited inside the foundation stone under the pillar of a pagoda at {{ill|Asuka-dera|en|Asuka-dera|WD=Q779755|lt=Asuka-dera|1=Asuka-dera}} (Hōkō-ji at the time), a temple whose construction Umako ordered, according to the Suiko section of the [[Nihon Shoki|Nihonshoki]].<ref>{{cite book |author=Aston, W. G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }}</ref>
[[Ishibutai Kofun]] is believed to be the tomb of Soga no Umako.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.asukanet.gr.jp/asukahome/ASUKA2/ASUKAKOFUN/isibutaiK.html |title=Ishibutai kofun |publisher=Asukanet.gr.jp |access-date=2012-06-10}}</ref>
==Genealogy== Soga no Umako's wife was a daughter of Mononobe no Ogushi and a sister of Mononobe no Moriya; they had five children.
*{{ill|Soga no Emishi|en|Soga no Emishi|WD=Q1292368|lt=Soga no Emishi|1=Soga no Emishi}} *Soga no Kuramaro *Kahakami no Iratsume, made consort of {{ill|Emperor Sushun|en|Emperor Sushun|WD=Q349238|lt=Emperor Sushun|1=Emperor Sushun}} in 587, married to Yamato no Aya no Atahe in 592. *Tojiko no Iratsume, consort of [[:en:Shotoku Taishi|Shotoku Taishi]] *Hode no Iratsume, consort of {{ill|Emperor Jomei|en|Emperor Jomei|WD=Q329761|lt=Emperor Jomei|1=Emperor Jomei}}
==References== <references/>]]{{Japan-bio-stub}}

Sharpe |location=Armonk, N.Y |year=1991 |page=40 |isbn=0-87332-552-4 }}</ref> and established the Soga clan's stronghold in the government by having his daughters married to members of the imperial family.
In the late 6th century, Soga no Umako went to great lengths to promote Buddhism in Japan, and was instrumental in its acceptance. G. |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times |publisher=Cosimo, Inc. |location=New York |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60520-146-7 }}</ref>
